title: Present perfect simple, present perfect continuous
description: Learn how to use the present perfect simple and present perfect continuous to describe past actions connected to the present.

topics.title: Present perfect simple
topics.description: Used for situations that started in the past and are still true, completed actions at an unspecified past time, and actions whose results are important now.

uses.usage: Situations that started in the past and are still true
uses.example: Mrs Jenkins has been the head teacher for three years.

uses.usage: Completed actions at a time in the past which is not mentioned
Completed actions where the important thing is the result now

notes.content: The present perfect simple is often used with the following words and phrases.
examples: For: She's taught German here for over five years.
Since: Mr Gray has taught French here since 2006.
Just: We've just done this exercise.
Already: We've already done this exercise.
Yet: We haven't checked the answers yet.
Ever: Have you ever had guitar lessons?
Never: I've never understood why they give us so much homework!
It's the first time: It's the first time we've watched a video in class.

notes.content: We do not use the present perfect simple when we want to say exactly when something happened in the past. We use the past simple instead.
examples: Correct: I did my homework last night.
Incorrect: I've done my homework last night.

notes.content: We do not use the past simple when we want to show that something happened before now or is still important now. We use the present perfect simple.
examples: I've finished! Can I go home now?

notes.content: Some verbs have irregular past participle forms. See page 182.

topics.title: Present perfect continuous
topics.description: Used for actions that have continued up to now or stopped only a short time before now.

uses.example: We've been doing grammar exercises for over an hour. Can we have a break now?

uses.usage: Actions that stopped only a short time before now
uses.example: They're having a break now because they've been working so hard.

notes.content: The present perfect continuous is often used with the following words.
examples: For: I've been learning English for over three years.
Since: He's been learning Chinese since 2004.
Just: I've just been reading the school newspaper.

notes.content: The present perfect simple often emphasises the result of an action.
examples: She's written an article for the school newspaper.
This means that she has finished the article.

notes.content: The present perfect continuous often emphasises the action and the time spent on it rather than the result.
examples: She's been writing an article for the school newspaper.
This means that she has started the article, but she has not finished it yet.
